title: Spinning wheel oiling and basic maintenance
subtitle: A regular care routine for the moving parts of a spinning wheel: bearings, drive band, and flyer
excerpt: How to keep a spinning wheel running smoothly: where to oil the bearings, how to check and replace the drive band, how to inspect and clean the flyer and bobbin. Covers both modern and vintage wheels.
sourceNotes: Spinning wheel maintenance guidance is consistent across manufacturers' manuals and UK guild resources. The oiling points described are standard across most double-drive and Scotch-tension wheels (Saxony and castle styles). Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: The continuous loop of cord or rubber that connects the spinning wheel's drive wheel to the whorl on the flyer or bobbin, transmitting the wheel's rotation to the flyer. A worn drive band slips and causes inconsistent twist.

The U-shaped rotating frame on a spinning wheel that guides yarn onto the bobbin. The flyer spins around the bobbin as yarn is produced, inserting twist and guiding the yarn into the bobbin's orifice guide hooks.

A wheel tension system in which the drive band turns only the flyer, and a separate brake band on the bobbin controls how fast yarn is taken up. Adjusting the brake spring increases or decreases the uptake tension.

aliases: how to oil a spinning wheel

content.text: A spinning wheel is a simple machine with a small number of moving parts, all of which need occasional lubrication and inspection. A well-maintained wheel runs quietly and consistently; a neglected wheel drags, squeaks, and produces uneven yarn. This guide covers the maintenance routine for a standard Saxony or castle-style wheel with either
or double-drive. Most modern wheels (Ashford, Schacht, Louet, and comparable) follow the same layout; consult the manufacturer's diagram for your specific wheel.

Oil the wheel every 8 to 10 hours of use, or if it begins to squeak. A wheel that is used daily needs oiling weekly. A wheel stored unused for months needs oiling before the next session. Use spinning wheel oil (a light mineral oil sold by most wheel manufacturers) or a refined sewing machine oil; never use WD-40, cooking oil, or petroleum jelly as these gum or oxidise over time.
Apply one or two drops of oil to each of the following points: the maidens (the upright posts that hold the

) where the flyer shaft passes through; the axle bearing on the main drive wheel where it rests in the wheel supports; and the crank axle if the wheel has a crank. On castle wheels, also oil the footman pivot point (where the connecting rod attaches to the crank) and the treadle pivot bar. Avoid oiling the drive band grooves or the brake band itself: oil on the driving surfaces causes slipping.

wears gradually, stretching and fraying. A stretched band produces slipping, uneven twist, and a wheel that feels sluggish even at low tension. Most modern wheels use either a polycotton cord or a rubber O-ring band. When the band no longer holds tension correctly, replace it: cut a new length of the correct cord, overlap the ends by 1 cm, and join with a square knot. For O-ring bands, order the correct diameter from the manufacturer. A correctly tensioned band produces a slight resistance when pressed between two fingers at the midpoint of the span.

Inspect the flyer hooks (the small hooks along the arms of the flyer that guide yarn onto the bobbin) after every few sessions. Hooks develop grooves from yarn friction; a grooved hook cuts the yarn. Replace hooks by pulling out the worn hook and pressing in a new one of the same gauge. Clean the bobbin core occasionally by wiping away compressed fibre and lanolin with a dry cloth. Never apply oil to the bobbin core: it soaks into the yarn on the bobbin.
